coredata.dc:title: Construction of lanthanide-containing ratiometric probe for facile anthrax biomarker detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xiao
Qiu, Yu
Li, Bin
Li, Zhiqiang
Zhang, Ying
Wang, Jing
Xiong, Qingqing
coredata.dc:description: Since the outbreak of anthrax attack in the United States in 2001, much effort has been done to detect anthrax spores. In this paper, a simple and facile lanthanide-containing ratiometric luminescence “turn on” probe is constructed to achieve sensitive and selective detection of anthrax spores biomarker — pyridine-2,6-carboxylic acid (DPA) by simply mixing Tb3+, sodium polyacrylate as well as red internal reference which could exclude some disturbance from the luminescence of biological tissue. Our probe shows remarkable luminescence enhancement upon addition of DPA. The detection limit for DPA is measured as 35.8 nM with a wide work range of 0–66.7 μM. Besides, our probe enables distinguish DPA selectively from numerous interfering materials in aqueous solution. Further test strip experiments demonstrate the potential practical application of our probe in DPA detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide
Luminescence
Anthrax biomarker
Ratiometric detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Excitation (A, λmon = 670 nm) and emission (B, λex = 280 nm) spectra of BSA-Au NCs; excitation (C, λmon = 543 nm) and emission (D, λex = 280 nm) spectra of Tb(DPA)3 in the presence of ASAP.

(A) Emission spectra in the presence of DPA with different concentrations (0–66.7 μM) in Tris-HCl buffer solution (Insert: photographs of the probe solution before (left) and after (right) adding 66.7 μM DPA under a 254 nm UV lamp illumination). (B) Ratiometric calibration curve of the probe solution as a function of DPA concentration from 0 to 66.7 μM. (C) The 1931 CIE chromaticity diagram upon titration with DPA in aqueous solution (λex = 280 nm).

(A) Luminescence response of the probe solution in the presence of different interfering materials at the concentration of 66.7 μM. The histogram of enhancement of luminescence intensity ratio (I
543
/I
670 ) in the presence (B) and absence (C) of ASAP. (1) DPA, (2) PA, (3) IPA, (4) TPA, (5) 2,5-PDCA, (6) 3,5-PDCA, (7) 2,3-PDCA, (8) 3,4-PDCA, (9) 2,4-PDCA, (10) GL, (11) AS, (12) 3-CP, (13) BA, (14) 2-CP, (15) 4-CP, (16) NAD.
The structure of various interferences in this work.

Luminescence response of the probe solution towards different interferences. (A) The red bars represent adding 66.7 μM analytes to the probe solution. The green bars represent subsequent adding 66.7 μM DPA to the mixture (λex = 280 nm). (1) blank, (2) PA, (3) IPA, (4) TPA, (5) 2,5-PDCA, (6) 3,5-PDCA, (7) 2,3-PDCA, (8) 3,4-PDCA, (9) 2,4-PDCA, (10) GL, (11) AS, (12) 3-CP, (13) BA, (14) 2-CP, (15) 4-CP, (16) NAD. (B) The red bars represent adding 66.7 μM analytes to the probe solution. The green bars represent subsequent adding 66.7 μM DPA to the mixture (λex = 280 nm). (1) blank, (2) Ca2+, (3) Na+, (4) Mg2+, (5) Al3+, (6) K+, (7) F−, (8) Cl−, (9) NO3 −, (10) SO4 2−, (11) CO3 2−, (12) HCO3 −, (13) PO4 3−, (14) HPO4 2−, (15) H2PO4 −, (16) CH3COO−. (C) Photographs of the probe solution after adding 66.7 μM DPA and analytes under 254 nm UV lamp illumination. (1) blank, (2) PA, (3) IPA, (4) TPA, (5) 2,5-PDCA, (6) 3,5-PDCA, (7) 2,3-PDCA, (8) 3,4-PDCA, (9) 2,4-PDCA, (10) GL, (11) AS, (12) 3-CP, (13) BA, (14) 2-CP, (15) 4-CP, (16) NAD.
The decay curves of the probe solution in the absence (A) and presence (B) of 66.7 μM DPA (excited at 280 nm and monitored at 543 nm).

(A) Comparison of the color of test paper strips impregnated with different analytes (66.7 μM) under 254 nm UV lamp illumination. (1) DPA, (2) PA, (3) IPA, (4) TPA, (5) 2,5-PDCA, (6) 3,5-PDCA, (7) 2,3-PDCA, (8) 3,4-PDCA, (9) 2,4-PDCA, (10) GL, (11) AS, (12) 3-CP, (13) BA, (14) 2-CP, (15) 4-CP, (16) NAD. (B) Color of test paper strip under 254 nm UV lamp illumination after writing by individual DPA solution (left) and mixture solution of DPA and interfering substances (right).
Schematic represent of DPA detection process.

head.ce:title: Construction of lanthanide-containing ratiometric probe for facile anthrax biomarker detection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Hepatobiliary Cancer, Liver Cancer Research Center for Prevention and Therapy, Tianjin Medical University Cancer Institute & Hospital, National Clinical Research Center for Cancer; Key Laboratory of Cancer Prevention and Therapy, Tianjin; Tianjin'’s Clinical Research Center for Cancer; Tianjin 300060, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since the outbreak of anthrax attack in the United States in 2001, much effort has been done to detect anthrax spores. In this paper, a simple and facile lanthanide-containing ratiometric luminescence “turn on” probe is constructed to achieve sensitive and selective detection of anthrax spores biomarker — pyridine-2,6-carboxylic acid (DPA) by simply mixing Tb3+, sodium polyacrylate as well as red internal reference which could exclude some disturbance from the luminescence of biological tissue. Our probe shows remarkable luminescence enhancement upon addition of DPA. The detection limit for DPA is measured as 35.8 nM with a wide work range of 0–66.7 μM. Besides, our probe enables distinguish DPA selectively from numerous interfering materials in aqueous solution. Further test strip experiments demonstrate the potential practical application of our probe in DPA detection.

We report a lanthanide-containing ratiometric probe for anthrax biomarker detection with high sensitivity, excellent selectivity and potential practical application.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: To date, the harm of Bacillus anthrax to social public heath still accounts for a considerable proportion [1,2]. Bacillus anthrax forms spores in extreme conditions, which are highly resistant to extreme environment because of its low water content, such as high temperature, UV radiation, ionizing radiation and a variety of toxic chemicals [3,4]. In the process of forming spores, DPA (pyridine-2,6-dicarboxylic acid) is generated randomly, accounting for 5–15% of the dry mass of spore [5,6]. As the major and unique component of bacterial spores, it is not found in other natural or artificial substances, and thus considered to be an ideal biomarker of Bacillus anthrax spores [7]. Therefore, the detection of DPA is of prime importance in studying the formation and bacterial detection [2]. Although a variety of measurement strategies have been developed, such as immunoassays [8], polymerase chain reactions [9], surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [10], their practical application is limited by the complicated operation and professional analysis [11].

Compared to other detection signals, luminescence from lanthanide (Ln) ions assays owns high sensitivity for the detection of DPA due to the high affinity binding of DPA to Ln3+ and the unique optical properties of Ln [12–17]. Luminescence of Ln3+ is often not strong because of forbidden f-f transition [18]. The matched lowest triplet energy level of DPA is able to transfer its energy to Ln3+ to emit their own characteristic luminescence via “antenna effect” [19,20], through which luminescence “turn on” system could be constructed. However, eliminating interference from interfering materials with similar structure is really great challenge in detection of DPA. To overcome this drawback, plenty of well-designed dual-emission ratiometric luminescence platforms such as nanoparticles [6], micelles [21], metal-organic frameworks [22] have been developed, which can eliminate the unavoidably potential errors, arising from sensor concentration, excitation power, the potential operation and environmental effects [11,23,24]. However, the reference luminescence was generally in the blue region [19,21], which hampers their application in vivo detection due to the low penetration of UV and short-wavelength visible light in biological tissue [25–28]. We here choose water-soluble Au noble metal nanoclusters (NCs) in bovine serum albumin (BSA) solution with red luminescence emission as interior-reference [29]. The high structure and luminescence properties of BSA-Au NCs within the scope of pH of 3 to 12, in various buffer solutions, or in solutions with a high salt concentration enabling it appealing interior-reference candidate. In our previous work, we find that sodium polyacrylate (ASAP) used to exfoliate Laponite nanosheet will quench the luminescence of Ln-containing supramolecular hybrid hydrogels because of the competitive coordination of acrylate units to Ln3+ [30,31]. Excitedly, DPA is the only ligand which can sensitize luminescence of Ln ions even in the presence of ASAP [32]. Inspired by this, we deduce ASAP would be an ideal matrix to effectively block the interference from other aromatic analogues. In this paper, a simple and facile Ln -containing ratiometric luminescence “turn on” probe is constructed to achieve sensitive and selective detection of DPA by simple mixing Tb3+, ASAP as well as BSA-Au NCs in aqueous solution. In our detection system, only red luminescence of BSA-Au NCs can be found before adding DPA, because acrylate unions cannot sensitize Tb3+ although they can coordinate with Tb3+. When meeting DPA, characteristic green luminescence of Tb3+ can be observed owning to a highly efficient ligand-to-metal energy transfer from DPA to Tb3+. As a result, detection limit as low as 35.8 nM is achieved with a wide work range of 0 to 66.7 μM. Besides, our probe can fully distinguish DPA from numerous interfering materials in aqueous solution. In addition, the concentration of DPA is able to be immediately read out according to 1931 Commission Internationale d'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram. More importantly, test strip experiments further demonstrate the potential practical application of our probe in DPA detection (Scheme 1 ).

All reagents were used without further purification. TbCl3•6H2O (99.99%) was purchased from Beijing HWRK Chem Co., LTD. Sodium polyarylate, poly (vinyl alcohol) and benzoic acid were purchased from Aladdin. 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (99%), 2,3-pyridinedicarboxylic acid, 2,4-pyridinedicarboxylic acid, 2,5-pyridinedicarboxylic acid, pyridine-3,4-dicarboxylic acid, pyridine-3,5-dicarboxylic acid, 3-carboxypyridine, 2-carboxypyridine, 4-carboxypyridine and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd., Beijing, China. Phthalic acid, isophthalic acid, terephthalic acid were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., LTD. Aspartic acid and glutamic acid were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents Factory. All of our work were conducted in 0.1 M Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 7.0) unless noted otherwise.

All glassware was washed with Aqua Regia (HCl:HNO3 volume ratio = 3:1), and washed with ethanol and distilled water. HAuCl4 in aqueous solution (5 mL, 10 mM, 37 °C) was added to BSA solution (5 mL, 50 mg/mL, 37 °C) under stirring intensely. Two minutes later, NaOH solution (0.5 mL, 1 M) was added, and the solution above was stirred intensely at 37 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of probe solution and process to determining proportions
ce:section.ce:para:
Sodium polyarylate (4.8 mg, average Mw = 5000–7000 KDa) was dispersed in 4.9 mL Tris-HCl buffer solution under stirring for 50 min at room temperature. After that, TbCl3·6H2O aqueous solution (0.1 M, 0.1 mL) was added to the above solution, and the mixed solution was further stirred for 30 min. 10.0 μL BSA-Au NCs were then added into 3.0 mL of the above mentioned solution. This molar ratio leads to the most evident change of luminescence color. In order to determine the proportion of concentration of Tb3+ and ASAP, we prepared six mixed solutions with different molar ratios of ASAP to Tb3+ (nASAP/nTb 3+ = 1:1, 2:1, 3:1, 4:1, 5:1 and 6:1). As shown in Fig. S1–S4, we found that the luminescence effect of other interfering materials cannot be eliminated when nASAP/nTb 3+ < 5:1. As shown in Fig. S5, linear relationship between luminescence intensity ratio I
543
/I
670 and the concentration of DPA became bad (R2 = 0.9637) when nASAP/nTb 3+ = 6:1. Therefore, nASAP/nTb 3+ was maintained at 5:1 in the following experiments. The concentration of sodium acrylate unit and TbCl3 was 10.0 mM and 2.0 mM, respectively. In the on-site detection experiment, sodium acrylate unit and TbCl3's concentration was maintained at 200 mM and 40 mM, respectively, and 200 μL BSA-Au NCs was added.

The luminescence measurements (excitation and emission spectra, luminescence decay time) were tested by an Edinburgh Instruments FS920P near-infrared spectrometer, with a 450 W xenon lamp as the steady-state excitation source, a double excitation monochromator (1800 lines•mm−1), an emission monochromator (600 lines•mm−1), a semiconductor cooled Hamamatsu RMP928 photomultiplier tube. Absolute quantum yield measurements were carried on the spectrophotometer above equipped with an integrating sphere.

BSA-Au NCs was prepared according to the reported method [29]. The excitation spectrum of BSA-Au NCs aqueous solution showed broad band within the range of 250–450 nm (Fig. 1A), overlapped with the absorption peak of Tb(DPA)3 at 280 nm (Fig. 1C), indicating that the BSA-Au NCs and Tb(DPA)3 could be simultaneously excited at 280 nm. BSA-Au NCs exhibited strong emission peak at 670 nm (Fig. 1B), with negligible spectral overlap with the emission of Tb(DPA)3 (Fig. 1D). As shown in Fig. 2A, luminescence responses were measured at different concentration of DPA to evaluate the sensitivity of our probe. Only red luminescence of BSA-Au NCs at 670 nm could be observed before adding DPA (Fig. S7). When increasing the concentration of DPA, the luminescence intensity of Tb3+ increased while the luminescence intensity of internal reference at 670 nm remained unchanged. A good linear relationship between the emission intensity ratio I
543
/I
670 and the concentration of DPA was obtained over a wide range from 0 to 66.7 μM with R2 = 0.9985 in our detection system (Fig. 2B). According to signal-to-noise ratio of 3 [33], the limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 35.8 nM, which was significantly lower than the infectious dosage of anthrax spores (6 × 10−5 M) for human being [18]. More interestingly, it was easy to recognize the change of color by naked eyes when exposed to 254 nm UV lamp illumination. Red luminescence was observed before DPA was added and green luminescence after 66.7 μM DPA was added. What's more, the concentration of DPA could be analyzed from the 1931 CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 2C). In addition, I
543
/I
670 slightly changed within the range of pH from 3 to 9. However, it decreased significantly at higher pH (10-11) because terbium hydroxide occurred (Fig. S8) [33].

Moreover, anti-interference capability of this probe was also tested. To begin with, when 66.7 μM interfering materials were individually added into this probe, no obvious changes of luminescence were observed. Then the same concentration of DPA was added into the above mentioned mixtures. As shown in Fig. 5A, it was observed that the luminescence enhancement of each interfering substance after adding DPA were almost the same, and the luminescence intensity was also nearly equal to the solution only adding DPA. This means that the detection to DPA will not be interfered even if there are interfering materials in the solution. Additionally, it was easy to recognize the contradistinction of color by naked eyes under 254 nm UV lamp illumination, while no color change was observed when interfering materials were added, however, the probe luminescence changed from red to green when DPA was added (Fig. 5C). In other words, only DPA induced a “turn on” response. All of these results proved that our probe shows high selectivity and excellent anti-interference capability towards DPA in aqueous solution. In addition, in order to demonstrate the universality of our probe common cations and anions including Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, F−, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, PO4 3−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −, CH3COO− were selected as co-existence with DPA in Fig. 5B. Firstly, 66.7 μM cations and anions were added into our probe solution and no noticeable change was obtained. Secondly, the concentrations of these cations and anions were the same as DPA, these cations as well as anions had negligible emission intensity change compared to individual DPA, suggesting our probe still have good anti-interference capability in the presence of common ions.

The high selectivity of our probe can be attributed to the following reasons. When interfering materials were added, the H2O and acrylate units coordinated with Tb3+ because of its prior affinity for Ln ions. However, the luminescence of the Tb3+ greatly enhanced when DPA was added because the triplet state energy of DPA mostly matched with the excited state energy of Tb3+ [30,34]. Thus, Tb3+ can only be sensitized by DPA in the presence of ASAP and water. In order to prove our viewpoint, some control experiments were carried out. When interfering materials were added into Tb3+ buffer solution without ASAP, significant enhanced luminescence was obtained (Fig. 3C). In addition, as shown in Fig. S9, we used poly (vinyl alcohol) to replace ASAP to produce our probe. Significant enhanced luminescence was also obtained, confirming the important role of carboxylate ions. These results confirmed interfering materials could coordinate with Tb3+ without acrylate units of ASAP, which is crucial to the selectivity of our probe. As shown in Fig. 6 , the quantum efficiency and luminescence lifetime of our probe in the solution before and after adding 66.7 μM DPA were measured and the results are listed in Table S1. Noticeable longer luminescence lifetime and enhanced quantum efficiencies were observed when 66.7 μM DPA was added. In addition, coordinated H2O in the solution with or without ASAP was also calculated to further prove of our viewpoint according to the literature method [35]. The number of coordinated H2O molecules is 5.27 and 1.71 in the absence and presence of ASAP, respectively. These results indicated partial coordinated water was replaced by ASAP. Thus, prior affinity for Ln ions of ASAP could shield Tb3+ from other aromatic ligands, amino acids, which is crucial to the selectivity of Ln-containing ratiometric probe.

Encouraged by the excellent “turn-on” behavior, especially the good selectivity and strong anti-interference capability towards DPA, we further explored the possibility of our sensing probe as on-site detection kit. Several parallel ready-for-test strips were prepared as follows: filter paper strip was immersed in the probe solution and dried completely. Then, the filter paper strips were immersed in DPA solution with different concentrations and observed under 254 nm UV lamp. The concentration of DPA aqueous solution was 0, 20, 40, 66.7, 150, and 300 μM, respectively. As shown in Fig. S10, the green color of the filter paper strip is clear enough to identify DPA when the concentration of DPA was increased to 66.7 μM. The completely dry filter paper strips showed red luminescence of BSA-Au NCs under 254 nm UV lamp illumination, then different analytes solutions (66.7 μM) were loaded onto the ready-for-test strips and observed under 254 nm UV lamp. As shown in Fig. 7A, only test strip impregnated to DPA solution exhibited green color whereas others showed no significant change. Additionally, we wrote letters “hebut” by only DPA solution or mixed solution including DPA as well as interfering materials. The letters “hebut” written by only DPA solution or mixed solution with DPA as well as interfering materials solution in filter paper all showed green color under 254 nm UV lamp (Fig. 7B). Therefore, the on-site detection kit had good anti-interference capability upon addition of interfering materials. Thus, our probe solution could be used as ready-for-test strips for detection to anthrax spore.

In conclusion, a Ln-based ratiometric luminescence probe was prepared for simple and facile detection to Bacillus anthrax by simply mixing TbCl3, ASAP and internal luminescence reference which is easier to penetrate animal bodies in water. Our probe shows remarkable luminescence enhancements of Tb3+ upon increasing concentration of DPA while emission intensity of BSA-Au NCs at 670 nm remained unchanged. The probe shows specific sensing towards DPA with good linear correlation in a wide concentration range. Besides, our probe enables distinguished DPA selectivity from numerous interfering materials in aqueous solution. Test strip experiments demonstrate the potential practical application of our probe in DPA detection. The simple preparation, easy portability, high sensitivity (LOD 35.8 nM) and high selectivity of our probe have great potential for detection of Bacillus anthrax biomarker in clinical analysis.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Xiao Liu: Methodology, Writing - original draft, Investigation. Yu Qiu: Investigation. Bin Li: Investigation. Zhiqiang Li: Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Ying Zhang: Supervision. Jing Wang: Supervision. Qingqing Xiong: Supervision.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21871075, 21502039, 21771050, 81501575), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (No. B2018202134, B2016202149, B2016202147 and B2017202048), and the Program for Top 100 Innovative Talents in Colleges and Universities of Hebei Province (No. SLRC2019023).
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118541.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioterrorism. This time it was real: knowledge of anthrax put to the test

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Enserink, Bioterrorism. This time it was real: knowledge of anthrax put to the test, Science 294 (2001) 490-491.

Tang, Development of a rapid and sensitive immunoassay for detection and subsequent recovery of Bacillus anthracis spores in environmental samples, J. Microbiol. Methods 73 (2008) 242-246.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of an anthrax biomarker at molecular printboards

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Performance assessment of three commercial assays for direct detection of Bacillus anthracis spores

sb:title.sb:maintitle: PCR assay to detect Bacillus anthracis spores in heat-treated specimens

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Discrimination of bacteria using surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.M. Jarvis, G. Royston, Discrimination of bacteria using surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, Anal. Chem. 76 (2004) 40-47.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Europium-decorated ZnO quantum dots as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of an anthrax biomarker

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Zhou, Q. Zhao, K.K. Liu, Y.J. Lu, L. Dong, C.X. Shan, Europium-decorated ZnO quantum dots as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of an anthrax biomarker, J. Mater. Chem. C. 5 (2017) 1685-1691.

K. Binnemans, Lanthanide-based luminescent hybrid materials, Chem. Rev. 109 (2009) 4283-4374.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: From antenna to assay: lessons learned in lanthanide luminescence

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Water-soluble luminescent hybrid composites consisting of oligosilsesquioxanes and lanthanide complexes and their sensing ability for Cu(2+)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoresponsive luminescent polymeric hydrogels for reversible information encryption and decryption

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Loading photochromic molecule into luminescent metal-organic framework for potential information anti-counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Li, G. Wang, Y. Ye, B. Li, H. Li, B. Chen, Loading Photochromic Molecule into Luminescent Metal-Organic Framework for Potential Information Anti-counterfeiting, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 58 (2019) 18025-18031.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Europium-based fluorescence nanoparticle sensor for rapid and ultrasensitive detection of an anthrax biomarker

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Ai, B. Zhang, L. Lu, Europium-based fluorescence nanoparticle sensor for rapid and ultrasensitive detection of an anthrax biomarker, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 48 (2009) 304-308.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on terbium functionalized carbon dots for highly sensitive detection of an anthrax biomarker

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Chen, Y. Xie, A.M. Kirillov, L. Liu, M. Yu, W. Liu, Y. Tang, A ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on terbium functionalized carbon dots for highly sensitive detection of an anthrax biomarker, Chem. Commun. 51 (2015) 5036-5039.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric luminescent thermometer codoped by lanthanide and transition metals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Li, Z. Hou, D. Ha, H. Li, A Ratiometric Luminescent Thermometer Codoped by Lanthanide and Transition Metals, Chem. Asian J. 10 (2016) 2720-2724.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel terbium functionalized micelle nanoprobe for ratiometric fluorescence detection of anthrax spore biomarker

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Luan, R. Meng, C. Shan, J. Cao, J. Jia, W.S. Liu, Y. Tang, A novel terbium functionalized micelle nanoprobe for ratiometric fluorescence detection of anthrax spore biomarker, Anal. Chem. 90 (2018) 3600-3607.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A luminescent nanoscale metal-organic framework with controllable morphologies for spore detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Xu, X. Rao, J. Gao, J. Yu, Z. Wang, Z. Dou, Y. Cui, Y. Yang, B. Chen, G. Qian, A luminescent nanoscale metal-organic framework with controllable morphologies for spore detection, Chem. Commun. 48 (2012) 7377-7379.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric and colorimetric luminescent thermometer over a wide temperature range based on a lanthanide coordination polymer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Cui, W. Zou, R. Song, J. Yu, W. Zhang, Y. Yang, G. Qian, A ratiometric and colorimetric luminescent thermometer over a wide temperature range based on a lanthanide coordination polymer, Chem. Commun. 50 (2014) 719-721.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for rapid detection of hydrogen sulfide in mitochondria

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Chen, C. Zhu, Z. Yang, J. Chen, Y. He, Y. Jiao, W. He, L. Qiu, J. Cen, Z. Guo, A Ratiometric Fluorescent Probe for Rapid Detection of Hydrogen Sulfide in Mitochondria, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 52 (2013) 1688-1691.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient red-emitting carbon dots with gram-scale yield for bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, J.S. Wei, N. Zhong, Q. Gao, H.M. Xiong, Highly Efficient Red-Emitting Carbon Dots with Gram-Scale Yield for Bioimaging, Langmuir 33 (2017) 12635-12642.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Li, Y. Nakajima, K. Niwa, V.R. Viviani, Y. Ohmiya, Enhanced red-emitting railroad worm luciferase for bioassays and bioimaging, Protein Sci. 19 (2010) 26-33.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xie, Y. Zheng, J.Y. Ying, Protein-directed synthesis of highly fluorescent gold nanoclusters, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 131 (2009) 888.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Organic-inorganic hierarchical self-assembly into robust luminescent supramolecular hydrogel

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Li, G. Wang, Y. Wang, H. Li, Reversible Phase Transition of Robust Luminescent Hybrid Hydrogels, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 130 (2018) 2216-2220.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanoscaled lanthanide/nucleotide coordination polymer for detection of an anthrax biomarker

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Tan, C. Ma, L. Chen, F. Xu, S. Chen, L. Wang, Nanoscaled lanthanide/nucleotide coordination polymer for detection of an anthrax biomarker, Sens. Actuators, B 190 (2014) 621-626.
